questions.question: Why can any host on a flat L2 segment act as a DHCP server?
options: DHCP requires a login but any login works
DHCPDISCOVER is broadcast and OFFERs are unauthenticated
Switches block non-authorised servers by default
questions.explanation: RFC 2131 DHCP has no server authentication; the client accepts an OFFER from any respondent.

questions.question: A DHCP pool is 100% utilised but 90% of leases map to MACs that never appear in the ARP table. What is this?
options: DHCP starvation attack with forged chaddr
questions.explanation: Forged chaddr leases never ARP because no real client holds them; this is the starvation signature.

questions.question: Which switch feature most directly blocks a rogue DHCP server on an access port?
options: DHCP Snooping with the uplink trusted and access ports untrusted
questions.explanation: Snooping drops OFFERs arriving on untrusted access ports while allowing them on the trusted uplink to the real server.
